title: CAR-T-cell therapy treats three autoimmune diseases in the same person
html_body:
What matters in science |
View this email in your browser Friday 10 April 2026

Hello Nature readers,
Today we learn that engineered immune cells have tackled a trio of autoimmune diseases in one person, consider the use of a national database to record instances of academic misconduct and hear what it was like to be in the room with Artemis II’s scientists at each stage of the mission so far.
CAR-T therapy is typically used to treat blood cancers such as leukaemia, and it remains experimental for autoimmune conditions. (Ruslanas Baranauskas/Science Photo Library)

One CAR-T to cure them all

A woman with an ultra-rare combination of three autoimmune diseases has had no symptoms since receiving a single dose of engineered immune cells. Researchers used the woman’s own T cells to develop chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells that targeted a protein on her B cells — the culprits behind her trifecta of autoimmune conditions. The woman experienced no side effects from the treatment, and fourteen months on she doesn’t take any medication for her conditions, says haematologist Fabian Müller, who helped to develop the treatment.

Nature | 5 min read
 Reference: Med paper

41%

The percentage of wild mammal species traded for food, fur, research and traditional medicine that carry at least one pathogen that causes disease in people. (Nature | 4 min read)
Reference: Science paper

Misconduct database divides researchers

A US database of people found guilty of academic misconduct or workplace harassment could prevent such individuals from securing jobs at new universities without disclosing their bad behaviour, a Science editorial suggests. Such a measure is necessary to “make it much more difficult for offending scientists to evade accountability”, says Michael Lauer, a former official at the US National Institutes of Health and one of the editorial’s authors. But researchers who spoke to Nature are divided over whether this centralized, confidential list would solve the problem or generate new ones, such as the risk that the identities of witnesses could be compromised.

Nature | 5 min read
 Reference: Science editorial

Question of the week

Would you support the use of a database that would mandate that research institutions report instances of misconduct — be it personal or scientific — among their current staff, and check the histories of potential applicants before offering them a job?

 a  Yes — but only if its contents were kept confidential
 b  Yes — and the information should be publicly accessible
 c  No
 d  Something else — tell us at briefing@nature.com

Artemis II

My time in the room where it happened

On Monday this week, as four humans flew around the far side of the Moon, I entered the heart of the Artemis II mission’s science operations.

From a mesh office chair by the wall, I watched the faces of researchers I knew well: lunar scientists I’d talked to at conferences, met on field trips or followed during the days when Twitter existed. Many were women; many were early in their careers. The lunar fly-by was about to start.

The next few hours were a flurry of tension, excitement and, as Houston radioed to the crew aboard Orion just before the fly-by, “Moon joy”.

Read my full account of my time at mission control (6 min read) or listen in as I tell Nature Podcast all about it (14 min listen)

Alexandra Witze, Nature correspondent

Catch up with her coverage of the lunar fly-by as it happened on Nature’s live blog.

Notable quotable

“The purpose of humanity is joy.”

Ahead of the Artemis II mission’s return to Earth, astronaut Jeremy Hansen says that his experience of the lunar fly-by hasn’t changed his perspective on humanity — it has only reinforced what he already believed. (CBC | 6 min read)

Features & opinion

‘Seeing what you’ve forgotten to notice’

In her debut poetry collection, fisheries biologist Liz Renner chose to celebrate the organism she studied for her dissertation, the gizzard shad (Dorosoma cepedianum):

O silver-bellied keeper of the lake,/ Whose schools like drifting coins revolve below —/ You rise where sunlight quivers into wake,/ And churn the silt where hidden stories flow.

Renner is one of many scientists who told Nature why they write poetry, from explaining the complexities of their work to processing the difficulties they’ve faced in their careers. Writing poetry “slows you down”, says engineer and amateur poet Fionn Rogan. Poetry, he says, “is about looking seriously at the world and seeing what’s right in front of you, but you’ve forgotten to notice”.

Nature | 8 min read

Futures: Immolation

‘The Extractor’ learns that some things can’t be bought in the latest short story for Nature’s Futures series.

Nature | 6 min read

Five best science books this week

Andrew Robinson’s pick of the top five science books to read this week includes an argument for art as the ‘fifth pillar of health’ and a deep dive into the language we use to describe the natural world.

Nature | 4 min read

Quote of the day

“I realized I wasn’t laughing because it was absurd. I was laughing because it resonated.”

An online Chinese-language game called Green Pepper Simulator gives players the opportunity to assume the role of an early-career researcher trying to survive six years in academia. The game hit a little too close to home for healthcare researcher Lingling Yuan when the “mental-health bar was falling faster than my publication count was rising”. (Nature | 6 min read)

After a week in black and white, Leif Penguinson is enjoying the vibrant hues of the Gruta do Lago Azul (Blue Lake Cave in English), a natural monument in Bonito, Brazil. Can you find the penguin?

The answer will be in Monday’s e-mail, all thanks to Briefing photo editor and penguin wrangler Tom Houghton.

This newsletter is always evolving — tell us what you think! Please send your feedback to briefing@nature.com.

Thanks for reading,
Jacob Smith, associate editor, Nature Briefing

items.title: One CAR-T to cure them all
items.snippet: A woman with an ultra-rare combination of three autoimmune diseases has had no symptoms since receiving a single dose of engineered immune cells . Researchers used the woman’s own T cells to develop chimeric antigen receptor (CAR) T cells that targeted a protein on her B cells — the culprits behind her trifecta of autoimmune conditions. The woman experienced no side effects from the treatment, and fourteen months on she doesn’t take any medication for her conditions, says haematologist Fabian Müller, who helped to develop the treatment.

items.title: Misconduct database divides researchers
items.snippet: A US database of people found guilty of academic misconduct or workplace harassment could prevent such individuals from securing jobs at new universities without disclosing their bad behaviour, a Science editorial suggests. Such a measure is necessary to “make it much more difficult for offending scientists to evade accountability”, says Michael Lauer, a former official at the US National Institutes of Health and one of the editorial’s authors. But researchers who spoke to Nature are divided over whether this centralized, confidential list would solve the problem or generate new ones , such as the risk that the identities of witnesses could be compromised.

items.title: ‘Seeing what you’ve forgotten to notice’
items.snippet: In her debut poetry collection, fisheries biologist Liz Renner chose to celebrate the organism she studied for her dissertation, the gizzard shad ( Dorosoma cepedianum ): O silver-bellied keeper of the lake,/ Whose schools like drifting coins revolve below —/ You rise where sunlight quivers into wake,/ And churn the silt where hidden stories flow. Renner is one of many scientists who told Nature why they write poetry , from explaining the complexities of their work to processing the difficulties they’ve faced in their careers. Writing poetry “slows you down”, says engineer and amateur poet Fionn Rogan. Poetry, he says, “is about looking seriously at the world and seeing what’s right in front of you, but you’ve forgotten to notice”.

summary.overview: 이번 과학 뉴스레터는 CAR-T 치료법의 혁신, 연구 부정행위 데이터베이스, 시집 출간, 미래 테마 단편 소설, 최신 과학 서적 추천 등 다각적인 주제를 다룹니다.
covered_item_titles: One CAR-T to cure them all
Five best science books this week

article_briefings.title: One CAR-T to cure them all
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 3가지 자가면역 질환을 동시에 겪는 여성 환자가 CAR-T 세포 치료로 완치되었으며, 환자의 T세포를 활용해 특수 항체를 개발한 연구 결과입니다.

article_briefings.title: Misconduct database divides researchers
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 학술 부정행위자나 성희롱자에 대한 미국 데이터베이스 도입이 필요하다는 Science 에디토리얼이 제안되며, 학계의 신뢰 회복을 위한 논의가 이어지고 있습니다.

article_briefings.title: ‘Seeing what you’ve forgotten to notice’
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 어류 생물학자 리즈 렌너가 자신의 박사 논문 주제인 '가izzard shad'를 기념한 시집이 출간되며, 자연의 미세한 아름다움을 탐구한 작품입니다.

article_briefings.title: Futures: Immolation
article_briefings.briefing_ko: Nature 'Futures' 시리즈의 단편 'Immolation'은 'The Extractor'가 물건을 구매할 수 없는 존재라는 주제를 탐구한 작품입니다.

article_briefings.title: Five best science books this week
article_briefings.briefing_ko: 과학 서적 추천 리스트에서 예술이 건강의 5번째 기둥이 되는 주장과 자연을 묘사하는 언어에 대한 심층 분석이 포함된 책들이 선정되었습니다.
